title: CLAUDE.md
text: urbnindicators R package provides analysis-ready American Community Survey (ACS) data minimal user effort. main entry point compile_acs_data(), pulls hundreds standardized variables (raw counts + calculated percentages), generates codebook, computes margins error coefficients variation. Five-year ACS estimates ; tract-level geography (block groups) Lifecycle stage: experimental Repository: https://github.com/UI-Research/urbnindicators

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Tests use pre-saved .rds files inst/test-data/ reproducibility speed. test framework testthat edition 3. CI runs GitHub Actions: test-coverage.yaml (push/PR main) pkgdown.yaml (site deployment).
code: # Load package for interactive development devtools::load_all()  # Run all tests devtools::test()  # Run a specific test file testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test-compile_acs_data.R")  # Check package (full R CMD check) devtools::check()  # Rebuild documentation (after editing roxygen comments) devtools::document()

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Assignment: use = (<-) Indentation: 2 spaces Naming: snake_case functions variables Variable naming pattern: [concept]_[subconcept]_[characteristic]_[metric] (e.g., race_nonhispanic_white_alone_percent) Variable suffixes: _percent percentages, _universe _universe_ universe variables, _M margins error, _CV coefficients variation, _SE standard errors Documentation: roxygen2 (v7.3.2) markdown mode enabled Conditionals: dplyr::if_else() (base ifelse()) Division: use safe_divide(x, y) percentage calculations (returns 0 instead NaN) Iteration/Loops: use functions library(purrr)–avoid loops

title: CLAUDE.md
text: table registry central data structure defines ACS tables package supports. package-level environment (.table_registry) stores table definitions. table definition list containing: name - table identifier (e.g., "snap", "race") description - human-readable description acs_tables - ACS table codes (e.g., "B22003") depends_on - tables table requires (e.g., population_density depends total_population) constructs - (optional) list construct definitions multi-construct tables; name variable_pattern raw_variable_source - raw variables obtained: "manual" (listed explicitly) "select_variables" (resolved runtime via select_variables_by_name()) raw_variables - named vector ACS variable codes (manual sources) compute_fn - function takes .data returns .data derived columns added codebook_entries - structured metadata codebook generation 30+ registered internal tables.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Users can request specific subsets data: Construct-level table names: ACS tables contain multiple constructs. split separate user-facing tables: - sex_by_age (internal) → age + sex (user-facing) - nativity_language (internal) → nativity + language (user-facing) construct names internal names accepted compile_acs_data(tables = ...) resolve_tables(). tables/indicators specified: 1. resolve_tables() determines tables needed (always includes total_population) 2. collect_raw_variables() builds named ACS variable vector tables 3. tables’ compute_fn functions called 4. Codebook CVs generated returned variables 5. Tigris geometry fetched spatial = TRUE "population_density" resolved tables
code: # Pull specific tables (using construct-level names) compile_acs_data(tables = c("race", "snap"), years = 2022, geography = "county", states = "NJ")  # Pull by indicator name (returns the full parent table) compile_acs_data(indicators = c("snap_received_percent"), years = 2022, geography = "county", states = "NJ")  # Discover available tables, indicators, and variables list_tables() list_variables()  # tibble of all variables and their table names

title: CLAUDE.md
text: R/table_registry.R - Central registry: table definitions, list_tables(), list_indicators(), resolve_tables(), collect_raw_variables(), expand_codebook_entry(), register_table() calls. R/list_acs_variables.R - list_acs_variables() (supports optional tables param), select_variables_by_name(), filter_variables(). R/compile_acs_data.R - compile_acs_data() (tables, indicators, deprecated variables), internal_compute_acs_variables() (legacy), safe_divide(). R/generate_codebook.R - generate_codebook() (registry-based) generate_codebook_legacy() (AST-based, deprecated variables path). R/calculate_cvs.R - Computes standard errors coefficients variation. Parses codebook definition text strings. changes needed adding tables. R/make_pretty_names.R - Converts variable names publication-ready labels. R/utils-pipe.R - Re-exports %>%.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: compile_acs_data(tables, indicators, ...) - Pull compute ACS data list_tables() - Available table names tables parameter (construct-level names) list_indicators() - Available indicator names indicators parameter list_variables(year) - Tibble mapping variables (raw + computed) table name list_acs_variables(year, tables) - Named vector ACS variable codes select_variables_by_name(variable_name, census_codebook) - Filter variables pattern filter_variables(variable_vector, match_string, match_type) - filter variables make_pretty_names(.data, .case) - Publication-ready variable names safe_divide(x, y) - Safe division (0 instead NaN)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: add new ACS table package: raw_variables (manual) raw_variable_source (select_variables) raw ACS variables compute_fn calculates derived indicators using safe_divide() dplyr::across() codebook_entries structured entries (types: simple_percent, across_percent, across_sum, complex, one_minus, metadata) Add new global variables utils::globalVariables() call bottom R/table_registry.R Verify: devtools::load_all() list_tables() shows table; list_indicators() shows indicators Verify codebook: codebook auto-generates codebook_entries – changes R/generate_codebook.R needed Verify CVs: R/calculate_cvs.R parses codebook definition strings – changes needed definitions follow standard patterns Update pretty names needed (R/make_pretty_names.R – rarely needed)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Percentages must 0-1 bounded measures must meaningful, non-missing values least 2 distinct values per measure CVs reasonable tract-level data (flag >50 many tracts) Compare published Census Bureau benchmarks available

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Prefer Grep Glob Explore agent code search. codebase small enough targeted searches almost always sufficient far token-efficient. Use parallel Grep/Glob calls multiple patterns needed. Glob: find files name pattern (e.g., **/*registry*.R) Grep: find code content pattern (e.g., right_join.*geometries R/compile_acs_data.R) Read: examine specific file know look use Explore agent genuinely open-ended questions don’t know search (e.g., “authentication system work across whole codebase”). bug fixes, error tracebacks almost always point right file line — start Grep/Read.

title: Aggregating to Custom Geographies
text: ’ll demonstrate using tract-level data Washington, DC, comparing share population receiving SNAP benefits across areas.
code: dc_tracts = compile_acs_data(   years = 2024,   geography = "tract",   states = "DC",   spatial = TRUE)

title: Aggregating to Custom Geographies
text: illustration, ’ll create four quadrants DC grouping tracts based centroid coordinates. practice, join tracts meaningful boundaries like neighborhoods planning areas, group arbitrary numbers adjacent tracts form pseudo-neighborhoods larger populations captured single tract.
code: # Calculate tract centroids and assign to quadrants dc_tracts = dc_tracts %>%   mutate(     centroid = st_centroid(geometry),     longitude = st_coordinates(centroid)[, 1],     latitude = st_coordinates(centroid)[, 2],     quadrant = case_when(       longitude < median(longitude) & latitude >= median(latitude) ~ "Northwest",       longitude >= median(longitude) & latitude >= median(latitude) ~ "Northeast",       longitude < median(longitude) & latitude < median(latitude) ~ "Southwest",       longitude >= median(longitude) & latitude < median(latitude) ~ "Southeast")) %>%   select(-centroid, -longitude, -latitude)  # Aggregate to quadrants dc_quadrants = calculate_custom_geographies(   .data = dc_tracts,   group_id = "quadrant",   spatial = TRUE)

what: Comparing Precision: Tracts vs. Custom Geographies
title: Aggregating to Custom Geographies
text: maps show share households receiving SNAP benefits. Notice aggregating quadrants produces precise estimates smaller coefficients variation.   quadrant-level estimates substantially lower CVs, indicating reliable estimates. precision gain enables meaningful statistical comparisons.
code: # Tract-level map map_tracts = dc_tracts %>%   ggplot() +   geom_sf(aes(fill = snap_received_percent), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +   scale_fill_gradientn(     colors = c("#CFE8F3", "#1696D2", "#0A4C6A"),     limits = c(0, 0.5),     labels = scales::percent,     na.value = "grey80") +   urbnthemes::theme_urbn_map() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(     fill = "SNAP Receipt",     title = "Tract-Level Estimates",     subtitle = paste0("Median CV: ", round(median(dc_tracts$snap_received_percent_CV, na.rm = TRUE), 1)))  # Quadrant-level map map_quadrants = dc_quadrants %>%   ggplot() +   geom_sf(aes(fill = snap_received_percent), color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +   scale_fill_gradientn(     colors = c("#CFE8F3", "#1696D2", "#0A4C6A"),     limits = c(0, 0.5),     labels = scales::percent,     na.value = "grey80") +   urbnthemes::theme_urbn_map() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(     fill = "SNAP Receipt",     title = "Quadrant-Level Estimates",     subtitle = paste0("Median CV: ", round(median(dc_quadrants$snap_received_percent_CV, na.rm = TRUE), 1)))  map_tracts map_quadrants

title: Aggregating to Custom Geographies
text: precise estimates, can better identify SNAP receipt rates differ significantly citywide average.  tract level, large margins error prevent many estimates reaching statistical significance. quadrant level, underlying data produces estimates precise enough detect significant differences citywide average.
code: # Calculate DC-wide SNAP rate for comparison dc_snap_rate = sum(dc_tracts$snap_received, na.rm = TRUE) /                sum(dc_tracts$snap_universe, na.rm = TRUE)  # Test significance at tract level tracts_sig = dc_tracts %>%   mutate(     significant = tidycensus::significance(       est1 = snap_received_percent,       est2 = dc_snap_rate,       moe1 = snap_received_percent_M,       moe2 = 0.005,       clevel = 0.9),     difference = case_when(       !significant ~ "Not significant",       snap_received_percent > dc_snap_rate ~ "Higher than DC average",       snap_received_percent < dc_snap_rate ~ "Lower than DC average"))  # Test significance at quadrant level quadrants_sig = dc_quadrants %>%   mutate(     significant = tidycensus::significance(       est1 = snap_received_percent,       est2 = dc_snap_rate,       moe1 = snap_received_percent_M,       moe2 = 0.005,       clevel = 0.9),     difference = case_when(       !significant ~ "Not significant",       snap_received_percent > dc_snap_rate ~ "Higher than DC average",       snap_received_percent < dc_snap_rate ~ "Lower than DC average"))  # Color palette sig_colors = c(   "Higher than DC average" = "#1696D2",   "Lower than DC average" = "#FDBF11",   "Not significant" = "#D2D2D2")  # Maps map_tracts_sig = tracts_sig %>%   ggplot() +   geom_sf(aes(fill = difference), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +   scale_fill_manual(values = sig_colors, na.value = "grey80") +   urbnthemes::theme_urbn_map() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(fill = "", title = "Tract-Level")  map_quadrants_sig = quadrants_sig %>%   ggplot() +   geom_sf(aes(fill = difference), color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +   scale_fill_manual(values = sig_colors, na.value = "grey80") +   urbnthemes::theme_urbn_map() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(fill = "", title = "Quadrant-Level")  gridExtra::grid.arrange(   map_tracts_sig, map_quadrants_sig,   ncol = 2,   top = grid::textGrob(     "Aggregation Enables Detection of Statistically Significant Differences",     gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 12, fontface = "bold")))

title: Aggregating to Custom Geographies
text: Aggregation improves precision: Combining tracts larger geographies reduces CVs margins error. Better inference: precise estimates enable detection statistically significant differences otherwise obscured sampling error. Proper error handling: calculate_custom_geographies() correctly recalculates MOEs, SEs, CVs aggregated estimates following Census Bureau methodology—simply averaging tract-level statistics produce incorrect results. Flexible boundaries: function works custom geography defined grouping variable, allowing analysis neighborhood, district, policy-relevant scales.

title: Design Philosophy
text: Support geographies tract level . Block groups supported margins error block group-level estimates often large make estimates meaningless. , many estimates available tract level available block groups. Support five-year estimates . One-year estimates bring margin error challenges, even relatively larger-population geographies, tracts, zip codes, places counties. Support subset ACS variables. title: Out of the Box Social Science Indicators from the American Community Survey (ACS)
text: compile_acs_data() makes easy pull multiple years produce publication-ready visualizations:
code: df = compile_acs_data(   tables = "race",   ## selecting 5-year ACS data from years with no measurement overlap   years = c(2019, 2024),   geography = "county",   states = "NJ")  plot_data = df %>%   transmute(     county_name = NAME %>% str_remove(" County, New Jersey"),     race_personofcolor_percent,     race_personofcolor_percent_SE,     data_source_year = factor(data_source_year))  state_averages = plot_data %>%   group_by(data_source_year) %>%   summarize(mean_pct = mean(race_personofcolor_percent)) %>%   arrange(data_source_year) %>%   pull(mean_pct)  ## order counties by 2019 value for the dumbbell plot county_order = plot_data %>%   filter(data_source_year == "2019") %>%   arrange(race_personofcolor_percent) %>%   pull(county_name)  plot_data = plot_data %>%   mutate(county_name = factor(county_name, levels = county_order))  dumbbell_data = plot_data %>%   pivot_wider(     id_cols = county_name,     names_from = data_source_year,     values_from = race_personofcolor_percent,     names_prefix = "year_") ggplot() +   geom_segment(     data = dumbbell_data,     aes(       x = county_name,       y = year_2019,       yend = year_2024),     color = palette_urbn_main[7],     linewidth = 1) +   ggdist::stat_gradientinterval(     data = plot_data,     aes(       x = county_name,       ydist = distributional::dist_normal(         race_personofcolor_percent,         race_personofcolor_percent_SE),       color = data_source_year),     point_size = 2,     .width = .95) +   geom_hline(     yintercept = state_averages[1],     linetype = "dashed",     color = palette_urbn_main[1]) +   geom_hline(     yintercept = state_averages[2],     linetype = "dashed",     color = palette_urbn_main[2]) +   annotate(     "text",     y = state_averages[1] - .15,     x = 21.5,     label = "State mean (2019)",     fontface = "bold.italic",     color = palette_urbn_main[1],     size = 9 / .pt,     hjust = 0,     nudge_y = .01) +   annotate(     "text",     y = state_averages[2] + .01,     x = 21.5,     label = "State mean (2024)",     fontface = "bold.italic",     color = palette_urbn_main[2],     size = 9 / .pt,     hjust = 0,     nudge_y = .01) +   labs(     title = "All NJ Counties Experienced Racial Diversification from 2019 to 2024",     subtitle = paste0("Share of population who are people of color, by county, 2019-2024 Confidence intervals are presented around each point but are extremely small"),     x = "County",     y = "Share of population who are people of color") +   scale_x_discrete(expand = expansion(mult = c(.03, .04))) +   scale_y_continuous(     breaks = c(0, .25, .50, .75, 1.0),     limits = c(0, .75),     labels = scales::percent) +   coord_flip() +   theme_urbn_print()
